comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few moments away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my thirty-second published book, another volume for the Ancient Order of Druids in America. At the time I wrote it, in 2012, the AODA was shaping up to be the only surviving branch of the various esoteric orders and traditions that John Gilbert had passed on to me and my late wife, and so the two of us (both then archdruids in AODA) and the rest of the Grand Grove talked about what else we might be able to recover. Reactivating a branch of the Universal Gnostic Church seemed like a good idea -- but we didn't want it to be yet another Pagan ordination mill churning out certificates for people who just wanted to perform marriages and claim unearned moral authority. Long conversations resulted in the idea of a monastic clergy, aloing the lines of the UGC's own monastic orders, with a distinctive liturgy and training program.

This book was the result:  a discussion of the Gnostic Celtic Church, its origins, and its training program, together with complete texts of its liturgies.  Anyone can perform its communion ceremony, btw -- in this tradition becoming a priest doesn't confer any rights, it involves instead taking on responsibilities.  The GCC is still a thriving concern in AODA, and details can be found here. If you're interested in the book, you can order a copy here.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

This is a question for Robert Mathiesen or Winifred Hodge Rose or possibly anyone else who has some understanding of runes in the older Germanic languages.

Background: I regularly engage in physical training of various kinds and I like to row on a rowing machine. I like using the rowing machine because, if used appropriately, it can be made into a brutally difficult workout and there really isn't much of a way to game it. It can be an absolute gut check, with the only way to succeed being to drive through a whole lot of pain and discomfort.

A couple days ago, I was rowing and trying to set a new personal record. About halfway through a workout over 40 minutes long, I was struggling but still going. A thought entered my head, with crystal clarity, to take off the mjollnir that I have worn daily for many years. At this point in my life, I have experienced enough weirdness that, even though I've never received something like that before, I reached up, ripped the mjollnir off, and tossed it to the side.

Again with crystal clarity, I heard the voice in my head and knew it was Thor, telling me not to put the mjollnir back on until I beat 40 minutes. Just to clarify, I don't really have a rower's build and even though I am quite fit, I was questioning whether I even possessed the physicality to do such a thing. Nonetheless, I went for it with abandon. I drove myself to the point where I was physically ill and my muscles were giving out, and then pulling more. I broke my previous best record by a large margin. It wasn't good enough. I missed the 40-minute mark by over 40 seconds.

I was definitely distressed by this. I felt absolutely naked without the mjollnir, but I wasn't putting it back on. I had been challenged and failed to meet the challenge. I decided to take this in the spirit of uruz, a challenge that must be overcome. I refrained from any strenuous activity for the next 36 hours and prepared myself in every way I possibly could, reviewing technique, eating for substantial energy, etc. All the while, I did not tell myself "I will succeed" but rather that "I refuse to fail."

After my 36 hours of preparation, I set up my rowing machine, tied my mjollnir where I could see it, and put the figure of Thor from our family altar on a chair in front of the rower. I proceeded to do an absolutely viciously punishing row. Well before the end, when my body was trying to give up and I was refusing to let it, I had my wife pouring quarts of water on my head. I felt like my body was being pushed into full-on adrenal fatigue. My muscles were demanding to give out, but I refused to let them. I drove on and I finished with 9 seconds to spare.

First I had to recover enough that I stopped shaking and could stand without wobbling. Then I retrieved my mjollnir and as I put it back on, in my head, crystal clear, was Thor's voice, "Fucking SAVAGE."

Savage is not a term I use or think of often, but the use of this particular word was extremely synchronistic on several levels. I haven't gotten a tattoo in many years and didn't have any intention of getting more, but I have now decided that I am going to get a new tattoo to commemorate this ordeal.

So, my question for Winifred or Robert or others is this: I know that savage is derived from French originally and so has no Germanic etymological lineage to draw from, but what would the equivalent word be in old Norse or one of the northern Germanic dialects? The sense of the word that I am thinking of is "fierce, violent, uncontrolled." Could you suggest an appropriate word that could be readily written in the elder futhark, or potentially one of the other futharks?

Thanks,
HV

If I may, first off, hail to you for meeting the challenge!

Secondly, from Bryan Evans's Plain English, which is a wordbook with the goal of helping to find words of Germanish stock instead of French/Latin/Greek for writing English, from the bit for savage: "biting, cutting, rough, stinging, waspish, wild, withering, wolfish."

Of those, only "wolfish" truly seems to share what you're going for. So, let's take a look at some of the other words you gave.

From fierce: "afire, bloodthirsty, burning, fiery, hot-blooded, hot-headed, quick-hearted, untamed, wild."

From violent: "bloodhtirsty, bloody, heavy-handed, hot-blooded, lawless, a law to oneself, quick-hearted, reckless, threatening, unbridled, wild."

There's no bit for uncontrolled, but from control v.: "1) to do as one will, hanlde, have it all one's own way, have the upper hand, hold down/under, lay down the law, lead, lord it over, oversee, steer, hold in the hollow of one's hand, lead by the nose, throw one's weight about, 2) to bridle, hinder, hold back, shackle, steady, tame, throw cold water on."

You might also poke around the Anglish Moot wiki: https://anglish.fandom.com/wiki/Main_leaf

Or the Anglish Times wordbook (give it a tick to load, then use ctrl+f for any words you want to see if they have an Anglish word for): https://anglish.org/wiki/Wordbook

Hope these help you find something right!
Jeff

replies.title: Thor's challenge
replies.content:
A fascinating challenge, and congratulations for achieving it! I like the suggestions here, especially Owain's suggestion of "oar-bear, ar-bjorn." A nice connection with that is that "ar" also means "harvest, fertile harvest, the results of one's work." An important toast, as was mentioned in the sagas, that was to be held before a feast begins was offered to Njordhr and Freyr for "ar ok fridhr, harvest and frith / peace." So the great effort you made was your "oar-harvest," as would be implied by using "ar" in a name or title for yourself--"ar" would have a double meaning here.

Here is another thought. "Modhr" in Old Norse referred to a state of extreme power, determination, rage, courage, fury, all wrapped up together. The term "Jotunmodhi" refers to the Jotnar in a state of battle-rage, and even more relevant, "Asmodhi, the modhr of the Aesir," is used to describe Thor in his heightened state of rage and battle-frenzy--a temporary but super-powerful state.

"Modhi" also connects to Thor's son Modi / Modhi, and to the power of Thor's other son Magni whose name refers to strength, power as well. This word comes down to us in modern English in the phrase "do it with all your might and main (main from Old Norse megin, Anglo-Saxon maegen)." To cap this, Thor's daughter is named Thrudhr, Strength. These three, Thor's descendants or the Thorlings as I call them, have much to offer someone on your path of developing your outer and inner strength under Thor's tutelage. I've written something about Thor's children in "Thoughts on Thor and his Children" on my website HeathenSoulLore.net, and a lot about the power of mod and its roots in the Mod-soul in various articles on this website.

So, Arbjorn would be a fine word to choose, but you could also consider Ar-modhi as expressing the state of utmost fury of effort that you worked your way into here. I'm sorry I don't have the ability to type runic letters here, but I can name my suggestions for you, using the *Anglo-Frisian Futhorc.* Of course, perhaps you'd prefer to use the Younger Futhark as being more Norse, as offered by Owain.

For "A," use the rune Ac or Oak. The rune poem says: "Oak is for mortals on earth the feeder of flesh (= meat: acorns feeding pigs); fares often over gannet's bath (the sea, oak formed into a ship); garsecg (spear-sedge, a kenning for the sea) tests whether Oak holds aetheling's troth (whether oaken ship can withstand the sea and faithfully bring the sailors to land)." This rune is ideal to use, to express your "oar-modhi," your rower's power! Another important connection here is the relationship between Thor and Oak trees. Ac is shaped like the Ansuz rune, two downward strokes from the top right of an upright stem, but the top downward stroke has another line bending up.

For "R" use Raidho / Rad, that expresses strenuous riding: another reference to your rowing.

For "M" use Mannaz / Man.

For "O" use Othala: you have earned your "ancestral steading, heritage" through your service to Thor!

For "th" use Thurisaz / Thorn: a rune of Thor as well as Jotnar.

For "I" use Isa / Ice, for the shining solidity of your effort and deed.

(This website of Tha Engliscan Gesithas has good discussions of this Futhorc: https://www.tha-engliscan-gesithas.org.uk/written-and-spoken-old-english/old-english-alphabet-2/the-anglo-saxon-rune-poem/)

Hail your deed, and Gods speed your search for the right runic expression of it!

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Once again, to any who marked it, a happy Midsummer/Summer Solstice! To those who didn't, I hope you are spending your summer in whatever way you like best.

To Share: I've put together two rather rough drafts of the "All Father" prayer for my Heathen Rosary undertaking, after a long poetic fallow time: https://jpowellrussell.com/#two_rough_all_father_prayers

To Ask:

1. For any so moved, I would very much welcome any thoughts on the above-linked prayers/poems over at my own dreamwidth post for gathering comments on the post: https://jprussell.dreamwidth.org/20195.html

2. For JMG, or anyone else with thoughts, I'm reading a book about Odhinn (The Search for Odhinn) where the author (Shani Oates) uses the term "god-form." From what I know of her background and her general approach, it seems likely that it comes from Wicca and applies in something like the "all Gods are one God, all Goddesses are one Goddess" sense, but another commenter brought up the possible connection with Golden Dawn terminology and suggested I ask about it here. So, any thoughts on what that term might mean and any assumptions that might be buried within it?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
In the Golden Dawn tradition, a godform is the image of a god that a mage takes on in order to represent that deity in an initiation or some other magical ceremony. For example, in the GD's Neophyte initiation, the Hierophant represents the god Osiris, and visualizes himself as the god, striving to embody the consciousness of the god in the ritual. The same thing is done as a meditative exercise in esoteric Buddhism -- the practitioner enters into meditation, puts his hands into the mudra of a deity, recites the mantra of the deity, and imagines himself as vividly as possible as the deity.

I don't know the book you've cited, so I have no idea if that's what the author is talking about, of course.

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
Thank you! I believe that the author has a background in ceremonial magic, so it wouldn't surprise me if one or both of those practices has informed her use of the term.

That being said, from context clues, I get the impression that she might think that whatever consciousness is embodied in the ritual has no outside reality or "personhood" - the central claim of the book seems to be that "Odhinn" is a fairly late (late Viking Age to medieval, possibly post-conversion) creation syncretizing various ancestral and spiritual tutelary figures that was influenced by Classical and Christian ideas that there should be "a" God amongst a pantheon for such things, which she doesn't think earlier Germanic folks actually worshipped. She has a heck of a lot of interesting research, but her writing isn't always the clearest, and interpretations like this don't exactly line up with my current understanding of things, so I'm trying to get a handle on just how much salt to take with her assertions.

Thanks again,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
I would take it with a great deal of salt. Odin may not be an ancient god -- as I recall, there are good reasons to think that he was specifically the god of warband culture in the late Roman and post-Roman eras of proto-Germanic society, and had no presence in that society before then -- but gods can be born, just as they can die. Beings can also rise from a lower status to godhood -- a great many Chinese gods, for example, are ghosts who earned promotion by granting lots of blessings.

I think Oates is quite mistaken to think of Odin as a figure that emerged as late as she thinks, for what it's worth. He was clearly being revered before the great age of migrations, as you find him in all the successor cultures, with a name reshaped by language shifts -- Odhinn, Woden, Wotan, etc.

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
Thank you, that's where I've been leaning. Personally, I lean towards interpretations that He is very old indeed - Kris Kershaw's The One-Eyed God posits Him as the tutelary God of the Indo-European koryos (adolescent warbands that served as a coming-of-age rite), and various sources I've read make the case that his traditional association as the sky-father of broader Indo-European pantheons is as old as it looks, and not a later harmonizing with Classical influences.

One thing that seems to be a key part of her argument is that she believes Wotan/Woden and Odhinn have different etymologies that were later conflated, but I'm not convinced. She seems a bit ready to dismiss as "speculative" or "wishful thinking" those inferences from scanty evidence that support the line of reasoning that Odin has been worshipped in some form for a very long time, while being perfectly happy to make equally speculative inferences from scanty evidence that He was not (besides the linguistic argument, she argues that most early claimed representations are ancestors/genuis loci/other tutelary figures that later got absorbed by the conception of Odin as a "proper" God under influence from Classical sources, and then strengthened by later Romantic antiquarians).

As I said, I wish she were a clearer writer, because she's done a lot of research and has some very interesting insights from a spiritual/practitioner point of view, but sometimes it's hard to pin down exactly what she's trying to argue and on what basis.

Anyhow, thanks again. I plan to share my thoughts on the book when I finish reading it, for anyone following along.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
To refute part of what you've written about the book you're reading, Jeff, one can turn to reports of a recent (2020) find in Denmark, the Vindelev bracteate, dated to the early 5th century. (In contrast, the late Viking age would be 900s-1000s.) This bracteate includes the phrase "iz Wōd[a]nas weraz, is Wodan's man." The first part of this phrase seems to give the name "Jagaz." Whether Jagaz was a king, chieftain, priest, hunter (Jagaz may refer to hunting) is unclear. There are two other bracteates from the same hoard which probably refer to Wodan.

It's important to put the question of Woden's origins into the right time-context. Defining the Germanic societies as 'people who spoke Germanic languages,' note that Proto-Germanic language began around 500 BCE. The Proto-West-Germanic languages formed during the 1st century BCE, later developing into Old Saxon, Anglo-Saxon, Old High German etc beginning in the 5th century CE. Proto-Norse began during the 2nd century CE. The Vindelev Bracteate dates to the early 5th century CE, hence only a couple centuries after the Germanic languages began differentiating from one another. Surely the idea of claiming to be "Wodan's man" didn't suddenly spring into existence on the day the bracteate was inscribed; it must be based on considerably older beliefs which clearly include Wodan as a patron of influential persons.

Then there is the Roman habit of naming the Gods of other people's pantheons with Roman equivalents, resulting in Thor being designated as Jupiter, Tyr as Mars, Frigg as Venus, Wodan as Mercury. This happened during the Roman incursions into Germanic lands, beginning about the 2nd century CE. This period corresponds to the late Roman era that JMG mentions. Wodan was known during that time.

The point is: there wasn't a great deal of time *before* this period that can still be considered Germanic culture and society, as opposed to the Proto-Indo-European root culture (Jastorf, among others) from which it arose. So when claiming that Odin is a 'late' God in Germanic belief, that needs to be seen in context of the Germanic-language / culture timeframe. Wodan is not named in Tacitus' "Germania" (90 CE) but neither are Thor, Tyr, Frigg, etc. This isn't evidence that he is 'younger' than the rest of the Deities currently known in the Norse / Germanic pantheons.

Now, there's a separate question as to some of the characteristics and the status attributed to Wodan: whether they changed due to the influence of Roman and Christian cultures, and that's quite possible. There's certainly some question as to how long into the past he was considered the chief God, the head of the pantheon, and not only for how long, but how widely that idea was spread among Germanic-speaking cultures--probably not very widely. From what little is known of the continental Germanic pagan beliefs, I have not seen any evidence of a standard hierarchical pantheon as Romans and Christians view things. Instead, we see tribes each having their own patron God or Goddess as the one most venerated, with other Deities also honored as appropriate depending on seasons and circumstances.

One idea is that the Norse and Anglo-Saxon skalds / scops sort of 'promoted' Odin to kingship: as the patron of poets and of warfare (hence well-rewarded praise-poems for victorious kings) Woden / Odin was very popular with the skalds and with those they praised in their poems. It was in their interests to 'promote' their patron Odin to kingship or chieftainship. And what was spoken / written in the poems are what we have to go on today: history is written by the winners, as they say. The early Anglo-Saxon kings--even Christians--traced their lineages to Woden to substantiate their kingly legitimacy. (For whatever it's worth, I think the idea that Odin is Thor's father, hence more powerful and older than Thor, also stems from this time; Thor (though not necessarily his name) is unquestionably very ancient, stemming from Proto-Indo-European times, as I've written elsewhere.)

The settlers in Iceland wanted to get away from the hegemony of Odin / kingship, preferring Thor and independence, and the great Heathen temple at Uppsala placed Thor in the central high seat, Odin and Frikko / Freyr on the two lower seats next to him. The Swedes preferred Freyr, and considered their kingly lineage to descend from him. Roman interpretation put Thor in place of powerful Jupiter, Wodan in place of tricky Mercury. Just as JMG notes that Gods can be born, so also can they change their status in their pantheons. It is certainly possible that Classical and Christian ideas influenced Odin's position in the pantheon and added attributes that support this change in status, and that this happened during the later period, as the book you're reading says, Jeff. But I certainly don't think that Odin himself is a late creation, though his status and some of his attributes may be.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Two Drafts of the "All Father" Prayer and a Question on "God-Forms"
replies.content:
Thank you very much as always for your well-informed thoughts, Winifred! As far as I know, I was not aware of those specific bracteates, though they do seem to blow quite the hole in her thesis!

My own understanding is pretty close to yours. From what I can tell so far (only 135/419 pages in, with a quick read of the intro and conclusion before I started!), Oates takes the Interpretatio Romanum evidence as being seen as overly consistent by antiquarians from the early modern period on - in other words, yes, the Romans said "here's Mercury, and there's Mercury, and over there is Mercury as well," but Oates argues those would have been three different spiritual figures that the Romans smashed together, rather than reflecting a genuine commonality between the different worshippers involved. She also appears to take the idea that early Germanic folk did not have a "pantheon" in the Greco-Roman sense to 11, along with the idea that Odin was "promoted" by Skalds and later kings.

In other words, I think she raises several good points against the interpretation fashionable among some at the more systematizing end of things that the Germanic folk had a neat and tidy pantheon in the style of the Greeks and Romans, and that it was a straightforward continuation of PIE worship and belief, but then she takes them too far. But who knows, maybe I'll be dazzled by her forthcoming arguments in the rest of the book!

Thanks again,
Jeff

Shani Oates has been around for quite a while, and is a prolific author. She is the Matriarch, or head, of the Clan of Tubal Cain, in succession to John Evan Jones and the Clan's founder, "Robert Cochrane" (in real life, Roy Bowers). The Clan was one of the non-Gardnerian forms of British Witchcraft that emerged slightly into public view after Gardnerian Wicca caught the public's eye, and it is one of the "meatier" among those forms. Tubal-Cain, of course, was the first blacksmith in blacksmithing lore, which craft traditionally has had its magical side even.

replies.title: Re: Praying for people
replies.content:
You know, when I first heard about this I was a bit puzzled because it takes a skill of self-reflection and empathy to understand that what you think is the best, might not be the best for everyone; that wanting something for someone might be layered in selfishness and an inability to cope with a world that is not in your control. In particular I think this comes from our society, which doesn't give us many opportunities to interact in a functional, mutually caring community --not even most families are like that anymore. It clicked for me when I examined my life and noticed all the times people tried to push down my throat their life advice (different from suggesting it and hearing my situation) about the mistakes they made, instead of seeing if the situation even applied to me. When that happens it is not about you, it is about them and they being uncomfortable, not looking for your well-being but theirs, by attending to their ideas of what they think your well-being should look like. "You are not in love with me, you are in love with how your idea of me makes you feel" a movie says.

Even though there might be situations when it would work, say, a parent taking care of a young child, it is easy for the invoked force to push the person away to the other direction, in the same way a love-spell gone wrong does. Instead of they backing away from you, they back away hard from the outcome you wanted for them because that is a way of saying to ourselves: well I won't be doing what you say because you are not seeing me, so I will reinforce my self image by doing the exact opposite. In effect, this does the exact opposite of what you wanted, and if it was actually good for them, you just made it harder for them to notice... A lot of troubled teenagers with self esteem issues that keep on rebelling against their parents well into adulthood comes from this attitude I think: by failing to recognize the other person, well, as a human, not as a character in their story.

replies.title: Re: Praying for people
replies.content:
I doubt it jibes exactly with JMG's ethics, so take it how you will, but the church seems to have a couple of ways of approaching this. 1) We pray for very general things, all together, in the litanies: peaceful times, health, safety in travel, good harvests, wisdom of rulers, the good of our city and country, that sort of thing. None of those are *at* a particular person, but are general goods that affect us all. 2) We add special petitions: currently, mercy for those suffering from violent conflict in Ukraine and the Holy Land. Note that request doesn't specify who we think should win, how we think God should intervene, none of that. Mercy. God's mercy takes many forms, and only God knows enough to administer that mercy rightly. 3) We pray for individual people who have asked for our prayers, or whose consent for them can be implied by their membership in the church (such as those recently dead)-- people who are sick, or going through a rough time. And also church hierarchs, by name. I don't think we ask them, but they knew what they were getting into when they took office ;) 4) We pray for civil authorities, not by name but by office: again, mercy.

In addition, I've seen various elders recommend a modification of the classic Jesus Prayer ("Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner.") to be used for praying for others, particularly where the relationship between the petitioner and the person being prayed for is fraught in some way: basically, Lord, have mercy on (other person) and have mercy on me, a sinner. Or more specifically, Lord, have mercy on (other person) whom I envy, and have mercy on me a sinner. I've seen it recommended in an interesting variety of contexts: for people with whom you are in conflict, people you can't stop thinking about because of envy or betrayal (and you need help to settle that in your own heart), and, interestingly, for people you are attracted to, with the intention that God will help you sort out whether the relationship is appropriate or not-- keep you together if you're meant for each other, or end it ASAP if it's not good. None of this involves getting consent ahead of time, but the petitions are aimed primarily at *the relationship*, with the good of both parties (mercy!) intended.

It's important not to tell God how things are supposed to turn out. He's not a wish-granting genie. I'm not sure that consent is always possible. When I get the news that a relative has been airlifted to the hospital, I don't call to pester them about whether or not they'd like my prayers first (shrugs): they are all Christians of one sort or another, and it can be reasonably assumed. There are children in my life who are in gnarly situations because of dysfunctional adults. There's no practical way to ask consent in their cases, and I do still pray for them. I don't pretend to know what's best, but leave that to God and keep things general: mercy, for everyone involved.

I am sort of curious whether JMG's consent rule differentiates at all between prayers/blessings directed from one person to/at another, and prayers to a deity on behalf of another. God seems quite particular about free will. IIRC he has mentioned before that within religious communities, consent to pray for other members is implied as part of the package, and that there doesn't seem to be a problem one way or another with praying for civil authorities and general peaceful times and good harvests type stuff, as this is a pretty universal religious practice (and implies that general prayer for the good of all is acceptable?).

replies.title: Re: Praying for people
replies.content:
For what it's worth, thanks to the "Blessing Walk" practice in teh Modern Order of Essenes, I've been spending a lot of time cultivating the thought and feeling of offering "take it or leave it" blessings - in other words, "here's a blessing if you want it, but if not, I won't force it." When I do this, besides focusing on it being offered rather than forced, I also tend to keep things pretty general "may you be blessed" rather than "may XYZ thing happen to/for you."

For prayer, I've also tried to focus on praying for guidance on how I should approach a situation or person, rather than anything for him or her or specific outcomes. Even there, I tend to add some "CYA" provisions along the lines of "in whatever way is best for all involved" and "nonetheless, your will, not mine, be done."

Not sure how well these would apply to the metta practice, but it's been helpful for me in cultivating an attitude of kindness and blessings to others while trying to keep their autonomy in mind and respect it.

Jeff

comments.title: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Is there such a reality as the Akashic Record, and can it be accurately read and interpreted by any mere mortals? Or only by spiritual masters? Or even if it exists is it completely unknowable?

Also, your thoughts on what/who the "illuminati" actually is and how much control they exert over humanity? What about their alleged equation with the Annunaki and "reptilians"? Is there a secret "cabal" atop the pyramid that has cosnciously conspired against humanity in their own interests for centuries? Or to your mind is all this pure fantasy and "con-spirituality" propaganda?

Many thanks for the time and response!

replies.title: Re: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
replies.content:
Ah, the akashic record. That was the hypothesis behind one of the two great projects in the occult community in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The idea was that everything that has ever happened is permanently engraved on the akasha, the element of ether, and could be read by clairvoyants. Three generations of occultists worked hard on that, and unfortunately what they found is that material gained by clairvoyance is a random mix of accurate glimpses, projected chatter from other minds, and dream imagery churned out by the personal subconscious. Most occult schools dropped the whole concept once it became clear it didn't work, but the New Age movement never got the memo and is still pushing a failed hypothesis as revealed truth.

As for the Illuminati, they were a short-lived liberal political conspiracy in 18th-century Germany, which was broken up by the police. People looking for someone to blame for the French Revolution took that reality and inflated it into this notion of a vast secret society that has the world by the throat. The Annunaki were mythological beings from Sumerian mythology that Zechariah Sitchin redefined as evil aliens in a bunch of very dubious books; the "reptilians" were an invention of David Icke, who apparently watched too many reruns of that otherwise forgettable SF miniseries "V" when he was stoned or something.

Do conspiracies exist? Of course they do. There are lots of them, of various levels of power and continuity, and they compete with each other. (You might find this post of mine from my main blog useful along these lines.) But there isn't one big uber-conspiracy, partly because nobody bothers keeping up the conspiracy schtick once they seize power, and partly because the people who crave power and wealth are each other's worst enemies. They're always out to stab each other in the back, since that's the easiest way to get more power and wealth. Do you recall the politics in the original novel Dune, with the great houses of the Landsraad, the imperial house, the Spacing Guild, and the Bene Gesserit all playing against each other all the time? Frank Herbert knew his stuff; every ruling class is like that.

replies.title: Re: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
replies.content:
Yes that is helpful.

To follow up, when you mention that what various occult investigations determined in part to be "glimpses" of truth or reality, how can these be teased out of the remaining maelstrom of distortion a d imagination, and confirmed as such, without some higher perch or process that transcends and can adjudicate/separate what the clairvoyants believe they're perceiving?

Regarding conspiracies, Noam Chomsky has said that this label is applied by apologists of the existing order to discourage and discredit any real/critical institutional analysis...by focusing on the absurdly sinisiter and evil motives and behaviors coordinated by just a few ultra oligarchs and personalizing the injustices at the expense of inquiring into how the institutional imperatives and the system itself are inherently flawed.

I am not that familiar with Icke's work, but my simplistic take has been that he conflates the "dark occult" with occultism as such, thus reductionistically consigning the whole project of the latter to the "evil" manipulative domain. Also he doesn't seem to offer an affirmative path to sovereignty and freedom, thereby reinforcing a victim mentality and sense of hopelessness for a humanity charged with confronting evil forces much more powerful than we and tragically beyond our control.

On a related side note, any thoughts about alleged MK Ultra and trauma-based mind control victim/ conspiracy theorist Cathy O'Brien (and her late husband and alleged rescuer Mark Phillips)? She seems autentic and sincere, if completely deluded. Are her "recovered memories" hallucinations, imagination, "implants", perception of the etheric/astral realms she has mistaken for the physical?

Many thanks for this work and forum!

replies.title: Re: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
replies.content:
1) That's exactly the problem. It's clear that there are some true things, because now and again details come through that the visionary didn't know but that can be confirmed from other sources. It's clear that some of it is false, because it can be checked and disproven. But there's always a vast amount that can't be proven or disproven.

2) Chomsky would say that. I wonder if he realized that he offered a conspiracy theory to explain conspiracy theory!

3) Sincere, but nuts. I'm not a professional psychiatrist but it sounds very much like acute paranoid schizophrenia to me.

replies.title: Re: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
replies.content: There is another fold in the whole Illuminati issue. In Robert Anton Wilson's Illuminatus trilogy one of the characters has a jaw-hits-floor moment when he reads (in Francis King's "Sexuality, Magic, and perversion") that there might be *several* Illuminatis. This is in fact the case. Historically there was also a movement called the Alumbrados in Spain in the 15th century. There was also a Sufi movement called the Roshani (means "the Enlightened". Within the Western occult world there has always been the idea of a group of high ranking members of several esoteric orders that were in an Illuminati group. Circling back to Wilson's sci-fi parfait, his idea seems to have been that the quote REAL unquote Illuminati didn't care a fig about political anything. Their concern was Illumination in the sense of a spiritual awakening. The Illuminati that I was aware of was that way. People who were in didn't have meetings as such, or jewels or membership cards, and they certainly didn't care about political domination or wealth acquisition or anything like that. They were in because they had reached a certain grade in a participating Order or society and had attained a certain degree of awareness. I hope that the "REAL" conspiracy of the Illuminati is that of enlightened people who are trying to help other people become enlightened.

replies.title: Re: Akashic Record; "Illuminati"
replies.content: Just as there seem to be dozens if not hundreds of different Jesuses, each of them corresponding to a particular spiritual state from the most debased to the most exalted, there may well be at least as many Orders of the Illuminati, ranging from the most degraded to the most exalted states of being.

https://web.archive.org/web/20201204115322/https://approachingaro.org/the-wrong-question

replies.title: Re: Gnostic reveries
replies.content:
I recommend finding a translation of the Bible that doesn't flatten out all the different Hebrew labels for the divine into bland words like "God" and "Lord." Here's the Hebrew of the passage you have in mind:

ויאמר אלהים נעשה אדם בצלמנו כדמותנו

In our alphabet, it's "Vayomer Elohim naaseh Adam besalmenu kidmutenu"; in English, word for word, "and-said Elohim let-us-make Adam in-our-image according-to-our-likeness."

The word usually translated "God" here is אלהים , Elohim. It's a plural noun -- -im is one of the Hebrew plural endings, like -s in English. So it doesn't mean "God." It means "gods." That's who the "our" and "us" is: the gods, of which Yahweh was only one.

As for your second question, it's not necessary to bring in other deities there. Haven't you ever done something stupid and regretted it, without worrying about what other people thought about it? I'm reminded of a similar passage in a different source:

"the reasonable creatures of the Earth,
let them vex and weed out one another,
and the dwelling places, let them forget their names:
the work of man, and his pomp, let them be defaced:
his buildings let them become caves for the beasts of the field.
Confound her understanding with darkness.
For why? It repenteth me I made man."

You might find it interesting to read Raphael Patai's The Hebrew Goddess and Robert Graves' Hebrew Myths: The Book of Genesis, which have quite a few details to answer the question you're raising here.

I don't mean to pile on, but thought I might add to this. I am by no means an expert in any of this, but I have been reading a ton on the bible lately, especially from the academic perspective. I hope no one takes offense as that is definitely not my intended purpose. Just providing food for thought.

Elohim is used both in the singular and plural sense throughout the Hebrew bible. Hebrew, at least in the bible, does not use a "royal we" like we have in English. I think in this context is is plural, but I would need to do some digging. One must look at how the Hebrew is constructed to tell if it is singular or plural and currently my Hebrew is non-existent at best.

One of the academic arguments is that plural Elohim is understood as a "concretized abstract plural" of the divine. Whatever you might take that to mean.

There is also a case made that the plural use referred to a council of gods with El at the head with his "consort" Ashera. Some academics suggests that each of these "lesser" gods was in charge of a specific geographical area, and may have later been revered as angels or archangels. Over time Adonai, who seems to be one of the members of the council, ended up becoming the focus of worship and replaced El/Elohim (singular) as "god" in the Hebrew bible.

These have nothing to do with a specific theology of course. Truly, one can believe what they want about the creation and who or what was involved which is one of the reasons we see not only a huge number of different christian denominations, but Jewish, and Islamic as well.

I'm sure there are a number of perspectives on this specific creation story. These are just possibilities. I guess the question to ask yourself would be what changes if God is speaking to someone else? If that doesn't fit your theology, then maybe don't worry about it. I would suggest that the bible as it is today, translated into whatever your preferred language, from any number of manuscripts written in a number of other languages, is not perfect. (that could get me in trouble....) Translation is a difficult process and sometimes only approximations can be made from the original language into the target language, and the original concept may not exist in the same way in the minds of those in the target language. So translators do the best they can.

Just my thoughts on the subject, you're absolutely free to disregard them if they are not helpful to your path.

Regards,
Bert

replies.content: Thanks. This is another example of the biblical stuff that makes me certain that a) the idea that the Bible is inerrant flies in the face of abundant and  perfectly visible evidence, and b) any variant of Judeo-Christianity is just not for me. If one accepts the starting premise that God is omniscient and all powerful, how could God create a being that disappoints him? How could an omniscient God NOT have known the Temptation and the Fall were going to occur? And "Adam, where are you?" "Hiding, Lord." "Hiding? Why hiding?". "Because I'm ashamed of my being naked, Lord." "What?!?! Who told you you were naked?" I'm sorry, but, are you kidding me? Where are you hiding Adam says the omniscient God? How does THAT work? And if God is omni-benevolent, how does he punish anyone for all eternity for a fault or act that is finite in time, and whose effect is also finite in time? A Platonist or Pythagorean would have said such a concept of God is grossly impious. A God who regrets any part of his own creation is equally so. I'll add it to my list of reasons why I thing Genesis is bad science fiction.

replies.title: Re: Gnostic reveries
replies.content:
I am not the first person to point out that the Bible isn't a geology textbook (or a biology textbook, or a history textbook, or any kind of science textbook, or a geometry textbook, or a logic textbook...) and shouldn't be read as such-- it is very often as much about the nature of humans as it is about the nature of God. There's a lot of multilayered and quite fascinating stuff going on in that text, but applying current-year logic to it, and dismissing the whole enchilada as soon as you find something that offends your sensibilities, is not a good way to get at any of the vast store of riches contained therein. It is, of course, your own choice and if getting at said riches is not your goal, nobody's going to force it on you. I am a Christian, and I would not apply that logic to the Bhagavad Gita, or the Tao Te Ching (which I got a lot out of!) or even the Koran-- I think anything that people preserve for that long... they do it for good reasons, and if I can't see the reasons... the fault is likely in my understanding, not the text.

From the inside of Christianity, I'd say simply that... if you're not a Christian, I can see why that passage doesn't make sense! But if you are, then when you run across something that doesn't make sense, it is very often an extremely fruitful subject for meditation, and after two millenia, it almost certainly has had a lot of exegesis written on it, if you're interested in how it's traditionally interpreted.

replies.title: Re: Gnostic reveries
replies.content: A Platonist might have objected to an eternal hell (and there are variants of Christianity that do not include such a thing) but I doubt the Platonist would have decided the Jewish or Christianity deity is impious on account of the stories in Genesis.  The gods of Greek myth often behaved in very unflattering ways, and Platonists like Sallustius and Olympiodorus came up with very creative interpretations of those myths that went well beyond the crudely literal.  If you haven't already read it, I recommend "Essays on a Polytheistic Philosophy" by Edward P. Butler.  In particular, the first essay, "The Theological Interpretation of Myth," is very insightful on this subject.  No reason it can't be applied to Jewish and Christian myth.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG -
As I continue a daily practice, I'm slowly starting to face and accept how dissatisfied I am with various aspects of my life, most of which I sort of stumbled into years ago. Job, living situation, geographic location, platonic friendships and romantic relationship - most of these are things I've tried really hard to stay committed to, but largely haven't ever been happy with. It's a very bitter pill to swallow, and I think I've coughed it up several times in the past. Discursive meditation has helped me recognize this. There are parts of my life I do enjoy, but they are vastly overshadowed by the above.

In regard to changing one's life, I've heard you use the metaphor: "if you try to drive to New York and LA simultaneously, you'll end up going neither place." Several major changes means several major journeys, several magical campaigns, and I have a lot of work to do before I even get in the metaphorical car. But any tactical advice for someone in a situation like this?

Thanks very much

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. Start by identifying the things that you're happy with, and explore what you will have to do to keep those intact while changing other things. Then, using journaling and meditation, figure out what the problems are with each of the things about your life that don't satisfy you. While you're at it, see if you can work out how you contribute to making each of them unsatisfactory. (We all do this. If you want something better, you need to change the energy you're putting out in order to change the energy you get back.)

Then choose one thing to change. You and you alone can decide which one. Choose it, change it, and don't look back. Once you've changed it, assess what happened and decide what you want to next. Quite often, changing one thing will set in motion a cascade of events that will change many other things, so it's best to wait until the dust clears before you go on to make a second change.

comments.title: Energy centers and dream data
comments.content:
A quick question to start: Previously it was pointed out that the cardiac plexus and the solar plexus are similar but distinct energy centres and that people tend to naturally work with one over the other. Could I ask that their similarities and differences be expanded on a little? My chest/torso feels like indistinct energetic mush at times and anything that might clarify what is happening and where would be appreciated.

In other news: In a MM two weeks ago I wrote about a potential future data point coming from a dream shared between and friend and I; Well, here is my report

To recap, my dream

I was trying to pack up a car and head out on a trip but I kept forgetting important things; This was causing me to have to stop and turn around repeatedly. When I finally arrive at my destination I discover, yet again, that I've forgotten to bring very important items. I woke up before any resolution could be found.

My friend's dream, told to me on the following day

She had finished dropping me off at a remote location and was on her way back home when she realized that she'd forgotten to leave me something vital! This caused her to stop and turn around, but she woke up before she could resolve the issue.

General themes: Being mired by setbacks/ feeling unable to progress forward, and memory problems.

Blame the solstice/full moon and/or my own progressed New Moon if you must, but I was feeling rather emotional during the week and I reached out to this friend to talk. As it turns out, she and I were caught in very similar situations and reaching very similar conclusions. (Not unusual for this particular friend, by the way!) This is when the meaning of the dream came together.

I can see two parts to it: The first is a reminder that I'm not alone in my difficulties, in my setbacks, my frustrations, or the self-blame that comes as a result. "This burden is not mine alone"

And the second is a bit more personal - The isolation I experience can be seen by others, which is a much needed comfort for me.

But all of this ties into to a more general point I'd like to make about dreams. It's been my experience that dreams are corrective, rather than predictive. Passive recollection rather than active insight. It's like the universe says, "Try first, sort out the details later!". Which itself reminds me of a statement that I can't recall the origin of, but goes something like:

A confidently incorrect statement will produce more replies than a sincere question

So there you go. :) cheers,

Amber Tottering Nymph

Hello from a fellow Pisces NN Virgo SN :)

The nodes and their (possible) roles in reincarnation is a personal favourite topic of mine so I'd like to throw my 2 cents in here, for what it's worth.

I've found that houses the nodes are in seems to have the same weight and importance as the signs. One of the reasons is because the signs themselves cover such a wide array of potential meanings and symbolism that they wouldn't be very effective goal posts on their own. Pisces, for example, is universal consciousness, isolation,illness and addiction, dissolution, charitable love...so do you leave civic life behind and go live in a monastery, or get a job at a hospital, or...? Plus, with all 11 of the other signs active in your life, it's impossible to learn/master all of the aspects of just one of them.

From there, it's not hard to assume that the next life is likely to feature a completely different set of nodes and houses. It could be because in your pursuit of this life's NN you neglect or overdevelop unrelated aspects of yourself which will then need tweaking and re-balancing. Or perhaps a person is unable to pull themselves away from the draw of their current south node so it will be the same sign, but in a new house (or vise versa)

So as to the kinds of assumptions you can make about a past life based on the South Node....it's quite limited, I'd wager, but you can glean a sense of the "sticking points", so to speak, that you're looking to tweak this time around. (Aspects to the nodes would help to refine this but that's much too large of a topic to get into here, haha)

comments.title: Gremlins
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've read your book Monsters, albeit a few years ago now, and you included a section on fae, but I don’t remember seeing anything about the creatures known as gremlins, do you know anything about them?

I got interested in the subject a few months ago when reading about WW2 era planes, and the inexplicable mechanical and electrical faults, and seeming mid-air sabotage, the pilots would experience.

There are some explanations that gremlins made a good scapegoat for not directly blaming ones' colleagues for misassembling an aeroplane (and there presumably were some genuine cases of human sabotage as well), which may be partially true, but there are numerous credible reports by multiple witnesses of seeing small goblin-like creatures sabotaging planes in flight. Some witnesses even heard conversations between them, and found tooth and claw marks on broken wires and equipment.

My guess would be that they are a type of sky-dwelling fae that was not really encountered before the age of powered flight (for obvious reasons!). They did not take kindly to trespassers in their domain and sought to damage the invaders' flying machines.

I haven't seen anything about recent sightings or activity of gremlins though - perhaps the rapidly spinning, edged metal rotors of a jet engine are too unappealing for etheric beings to approach (as many WW2 planes still had wooden propellors).

Mr. Crow

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and others,

1) Could you comment on the privation theory of evil (neoplatonic/aristotelean variations) vs evil as a real force/individuated consciousness as Jung considers? Where do you personally stand?

Jung and Catholic theologian Fr. Victor White debated the concept of evil for years.

from wiki: "While White was a great admirer of Jung, he was at times very critical of Jung. For example, he criticised Jung's essay "On the Self", and accused Jung of being too bound to a Manichaean dualism. He was also somewhat critical of Jung's Kantianism. At the same time, Jung was quite critical of White, for example, over his commitment to the doctrine of privatio boni as means of understanding the problem of evil."

2) Related to this, a quote by jung in CW13:

“The evil spirit is imprisoned in the roots of the self, as the secret hidden in the principle of individuation. He is not identical with the tree, nor with its roots, but has been put there by artificial means.”

I'm very curious what he means here. What are your thoughts and do you think this is related to the Lemurian deviation stuff infesting humanity?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
My theory of evil is a variant of the Neoplatonic theory, and is strongly influenced by the writings of Dion Fortune. In this view there are two forms of evil. Negative evil is the basic inertia of the cosmos, and represents privation -- think of it as the basic bloody-mindedness of things, the tendency of all things to resist meaning, purpose, and value. It is especially common on the plane of matter, which is more recalcitrant than other forms of cosmic prime substance.

Then there's positive evil, which is the product of wrong actions by individual conscious beings. That is perversion, not privation -- something has been twisted out of shape and distorted until it conflicts with the divine pattern of meaning, purpose, and value. Neither negative nor positive evil is an independent force, much less a conscious diabolic being; one is the innate cussedness of things, the other is the result of free will put to bad uses.

As for what Jung meant by that remark, I have no idea. Do you happen to know if he expanded on it in any of his other writings?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Thanks! So do you consider the devil/satan type spirits to be symbolic creations or some sort of lower order emanations? I'm wondering how you incorporate demons into this cosmology.

On the Jung quote, I will find out more. It just struck out a lot and I wondered if you'd come across it.

Someone suggested Jung was not just a gnostic but had explicit past life memories of participating in orphic cults. That quote really feels like something related to Lemurian deviation or older, but I need to find out more.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Oh, demons exist. Beings vary in how perverted and depraved their wills become. Disembodied beings with very perverted and depraved wills behave like the demons of legend, so we might as well use the term for them.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Regarding the “innate cussedness of things”: my uncle used a great word to describe this: inanimadversity (inanimate adversity), which I also find very useful. He was a professor of physics, by the way, well familiar with the phenomenon!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: I like the word -- thank you.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
I don't have a copy of CW13 ready to hand, so I can't get right into the specifics of that remark. However, since it has to do with the principle of individuation (which for Jung isn't a concept or an abstraction, but an energy that drives toward individuation), I think there may be something else going on.

The process of individuation often enough manifests at certain points as something "evil", in that it upsets whatever settled forms (especially conventional ones) a person's life may have taken. Jung's understanding of individuation was that it was specifically not the same as becoming holy or virtuous or admirable, but that it often could seem quite the opposite. In this sense, the process of individuation often contains, or is, a "guilty secret" or some sort. This partly goes back to Boehme, for whom this configuration is given a cosmic significance, in that the individuating impulse in the Divine is a kind of dark, bitter fire that disrupts the primordial unity. On the individual level, one instance is Gerard Manley Hopkins' line, "I am gall, I am heartburn. God's most deep decree
Bitter would have me taste: my taste was me...."

(This means that the first stirrings of individuation are often "contaminated", as Jungians say, by the Shadow, which may have something to do with the fascination with demons and demonolatry that can arise in people when they awaken to inner energies. A more mundane parallel is the glamor of the Bad Boy or Bad Girl that can be associated with adolescence and puberty. Or, in other ways, with the midlife crisis.)

James Hillman's The Soul's Code is worth looking at in this connection. One of the biographical examples that's conspicuous by its absence from that book, however, is that of Joan of Arc. (Part of the shift from condemned heretic to saint had to do with the formulation of the concept of the "lex privata", "the understanding that private conscience, when true and pure, trumps public rules" -- including Church law.) (See https://patricktreardon.com/book-review-joan-of-arc-and-spirituality-edited-by-ann-w-astell-and-bonnie-wheeler/ )

But I will have to go dig up a copy of CW13 and see for myself!

LeGrand

comments.title: Sixteen opposites and a demonstration toward pluralism
comments.content:
Hello John,

In part through experimentation writing "opposites" on opposing sides of flat hobby sticks, I have come up with a list of 16 opposites, with 32 partial answers to the question: "How might I be of service?" ...

You might be of service:

with-action-sooner / with-action-later

with-consequences-sooner / with-consequences-longer-term

with-service-to-a-few / with-service-to-many

with-effects-more-tangible / with-effects-less-tangible

working-with-ease / working-against-difficulty

under-nature / beyond-nature

necessarily-as-you / as-anyone-could

as-individually-whole / as-part-of-a-family-or-group

as-an-initiator / as-a-responder

for-human-good / for-plant-and-animal-good

boldly-as-required / carefully-as-required

by-softening-perspectives / by-clarifying-resolve

with-efforts-noticed / with-efforts-unnoticed

with-constant-efforts / with-selective-efforts

while-aiming-at-results / while-avoiding-failure

for-the-world-at-its-current-best / for-the-world-as-it-could-be

---

Perhaps there are obvious YMMV magical uses (including the three-stanza "oracle" below) for such a set of opposites?

I am inclined to imagine a mundane use in defending pluralism:

Suppose one mixes sticks, then picks one at a time to form three groups of three -- three stanzas, so to speak. The result can be what I take to be a complex valid "stance". Remarkably (though unsurprisingly), one can flip every single stick to its "opposite" and the result is also a complex valid stance.

In a world which to some extent teaches toward lockstep conformity more than pluralism, I consider examination and validation of "completely opposite" stances to be a useful demonstration. At this link -- http://main.jvp.mm.st/sevagusta-opposites.html -- a small generator of stances and their opposites is implemented.

Jerry Van Polen

replies.title: Re: Sixteen opposites and a demonstration toward pluralism
replies.content: Interesting. Have fun with it and see where it takes you!

comments.title: Evolving personal mystical wisdom practice
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Not quite sure how to say this or what I might even ask you but things are shifting in a curious way for me.

- Reading C.S. Lewis' The Discarded Image and loving it!

- I am actually wanting to go deeper into older forms of astrology, now. Definitely, drawn to reading Christian Astrology and other older astrologers writings. Might even finish Warnock's Astrological Magic Course now!

- The Dolmen Arch is like a close friend I want to support and I enjoy our connection. Yet, I am not interested in magic beyond the daily SOP. For the SOP I am using the WGS version.

- A number of the books I have bought over the years are becoming relevant to this shifting I am experiencing.

Much of the above started swirling together around the time I started addressing the Greek pantheon in the SOP. With Athena holding both a quiet and strong presence.

So somehow I am in the midst of an semi-organically evolving personal mystical wisdom practice weaving together:
- a practice of daily meditation, divination, and the SOP from the DA.
- Robert Mathiesen's book Microcosmographia Magica which has helped me explore deeper and with a new perspective into experiences I have been having for quite some time.
- a growing appreciation of much older worldviews which are at the very least rather out of fashion these days.
- a realization that I have been enjoying experimenting with shifting my worldview since childhood and up to the present day.

Some questions I do have:
- I sense some interesting occult possibilities or paths might be available as one develops a facility in loosening and shifting one's world view. Just how far have people (who have maintained their sanity) taken this kind of thing? This might sound like chaos magic but I think it might be something else.

- If I were to do a daily divination for the coming day using transits and my natal chart what do I do if there are no transits for the day?

- Recently, my body has been letting me know it is aging. I will likely need to use a walking staff for mobility reasons. At least for a while. Are there some ways I can include an occult dimension to my use of a staff in my daily life?

Thanks for this forum and all you do.

-- Eric

replies.title: Re: Evolving personal mystical wisdom practice

I remember a long while back that you mentioned that while bathing in cold water is a good way to clear off etheric crud, that it seems to be particularly beneficial for men. In the industrial world, both sperm counts, and testosterone have been dropping among men for decades.
